title: Congee with Century Egg
description: This classic Cantonese congee, known as 'Pidan Shou Rou Zhou', is a comforting and savory breakfast or light meal. It features the unique, pungent flavor of century eggs combined with tender shreds of pork, creating a rich, silky texture that is both deeply satisfying and incredibly nourishing. A beloved staple, it's perfect for a chilly morning or a soothing remedy.

ingredients.notes: or chicken/pork broth for richer flavor
peeled and diced into small pieces
for pork marinade, plus extra for serving
for pork marinade, plus extra for congee

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice and Pork
instructions.text: Thoroughly wash the rice under cold running water until the water runs clear. For a silkier congee, you may soak the rice in water for 30 minutes to an hour, then drain. Slice or shred the pork into small, thin pieces. In a bowl, combine the pork with 1 tbsp light soy sauce, 1 tsp sesame oil, 1/4 tsp white pepper, and 1 tsp cornstarch. Mix well and set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, bring 10-12 cups of water (or broth) to a rolling boil over high heat. Once boiling, add the washed and drained rice. Bring it back to a vigorous boil, then immediately reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer.

instructions.title: Cook the Congee to Desired Consistency
instructions.text: Allow the congee to simmer gently for 60-75 minutes, stirring occasionally (every 10-15 minutes) to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom and burning. The rice grains should gradually break down and thicken the liquid, creating a creamy, porridge-like consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more hot water.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger
instructions.text: While the congee is simmering, peel the century eggs. Rinse them briefly to remove any residue, then dice them into small, even pieces. Peel and julienne the fresh ginger into thin strips.

instructions.title: Add Century Egg and Pork
instructions.text: Once the congee has reached your desired creamy consistency, add the diced century eggs and the marinated pork. Stir gently to separate the pork pieces. Continue to simmer for another 5-7 minutes, or until the pork is cooked through and tender. Do not overcook the pork, as it can become tough.

instructions.title: Season and Finish
instructions.text: Stir in the julienned ginger and 1/4 tsp of white pepper. Season with salt to taste, starting with 1/2 teaspoon and adding more as needed. Give it a final stir and ensure all flavors are well combined.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Ladle the hot congee into individual serving bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions and a drizzle of extra sesame oil. For an authentic touch, you can also add chopped cilantro or serve with crispy fried dough sticks (youtiao) on the side.

tips: Soaking the rice for 30-60 minutes before cooking helps it break down faster and achieve a silkier, creamier texture.
Stir the congee frequently, especially during the last 30 minutes, to prevent it from sticking to the bottom of the pot and burning.
For an even richer flavor, use homemade chicken or pork broth instead of water as the base for your congee.
Adjust the congee's consistency by adding more hot water if it becomes too thick for your preference.
Do not overcook the pork; add it towards the end and cook just until it turns opaque and tender, typically only a few minutes.
article:
## History of Congee with Century Egg

Congee, a rice porridge staple, has been a fundamental part of Chinese cuisine for thousands of years, cherished for its comforting warmth and versatility. The unique 'century egg,' or Pidan, dates back to the Ming Dynasty, reportedly discovered by accident when duck eggs were found preserved in a lime solution. The combination of the two, particularly with shredded pork, forms the beloved Cantonese dish 'Pidan Shou Rou Zhou' (Century Egg and Lean Pork Congee). This dish rose from humble origins to become a cherished breakfast and light meal across all social strata, synonymous with comfort and nourishment. It's a testament to Chinese culinary ingenuity, transforming simple ingredients into a dish of profound flavor and cultural significance, often found in bustling dim sum houses and quiet home kitchens alike.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Congee with Century Egg achieves its authentic appeal through a careful balance of technique and ingredients. The long simmering process, aided by pre-soaked rice, ensures a wonderfully smooth and velvety texture, where the rice grains have fully broken down. The distinct, earthy, and umami-rich flavor of the century egg provides a unique depth, perfectly complemented by the savory, tender marinated pork. The pork's cornstarch marinade is crucial, locking in moisture and ensuring it remains succulent rather than tough. Finally, the fresh ginger and white pepper aren't mere garnishes; they provide a subtle, aromatic warmth that cuts through the richness, creating a harmonious and deeply satisfying flavor profile that is unmistakably authentic Chinese.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For truly exceptional Congee with Century Egg, start with good quality jasmine rice; its aromatic properties enhance the dish. The key to a silky texture is consistent, gentle simmering and frequent stirring, especially from the bottom of the pot, to prevent sticking and ensure even breakdown of the rice. When adding the pork, ensure your congee is at a good simmer, and cook it quickly until just done to maintain its tenderness; overcooked pork becomes tough. Don't be shy with the fresh ginger and white pepper; they are essential aromatics that provide balance and warmth. Always taste and adjust your seasoning with salt at the very end. Finally, a drizzle of high-quality sesame oil and a generous sprinkle of fresh green onions upon serving are non-negotiable for that authentic finish.

## Serving Suggestions

Congee with Century Egg is a versatile dish that can be enjoyed in many ways. Traditionally, it's often paired with 'youtiao' (Chinese fried dough sticks), which are perfect for dipping into the warm, savory porridge. For an added textural contrast and flavor, serve it alongside a small dish of crispy fried peanuts, pickled radish, or preserved vegetables ('zha cai'). Some prefer a dash of light soy sauce or a few drops of chili oil on the side to customize the flavor. In a full Chinese breakfast spread, it complements other dim sum items like steamed dumplings or turnip cake beautifully. And for those seeking comfort, a simple bowl on its own, perhaps with a cup of hot Chinese tea, is a truly restorative experience.
